sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, my-- my father's store was taken away from him.
Literally, he was kicked out.
Of course, his car was taken away from him.
He had a Pontiac, [LAUGHS] I remember.
And then he was--
He just had to bring it over to the Germans.
He had to deliver it-- deliver it in.
He wasn't allowed to drive it anymore.
It was literally-- they said, well, that's your car?
Not anymore.
Now it belongs to the-- to the-- to Germany.
Oh, yeah.
I was still going to school.
But one of the things that happened was that he was picked up by the Germans.
I guess the Germans thought he was of some importance because he had a store.
So they picked him up, and they put him in jail with hundreds of other Dutch businesspeople.
And I remember him coming back from that jail after about-- he was there for about 10 days.
And he had to do exercises.
And my father was not a very physical type person, that could do exercise.
And so he had to exercise and do things that he had never done before.
And he was madder than hell about the whole situation.
And he said, I don't want this ever to happen to me again.
And I think this was one of the things that also prompted him to find a place to go underground.
How he found a place, I don't know.
Again, I was too young to understand that, at that time.
No.
No.
I really don't remember that.
No.
No, he had to go there.
They told him to come to-- you see, they-- it was very insidious the way they worked it.
They said, well, we want to talk to you, and please come over there.
And bit by bit, they said, well why don't you stay there for a while?
Then all of a sudden, he finds himself locked up.
It wasn't done like that they picked him up.
No, that happened at a later time, when they picked us all up and we were given away.
No.
They just came there and said, OK, just tell us a few things about this.
And, well, I have nothing to tell.
OK.
Well, go in there for a moment.
Then they would close the door, and he was locked up.
Then he finds himself locked up with a few hundred other people who are in the same boat.
So he could read-- he could read between the lines, what they were doing.
They were, bit by bit, they were turning up the heat or the pressure.

So he said, before they turn up the pressure too much, that I can't jump out of the-- figuratively speaking-- pot anymore, I want to be out of the pot before that time, which he did by going underground.
My mother didn't believe him.
She said, ah, they're not going to do anything to us.
And my father said, I've lived in Germany.
I know what they're doing here.
I know what they've done to the Jews in Germany.
Let's get the hell out of here.

And eventually, then, about a month later, my mother and sister followed, understanding that, hey, it was getting too dangerous, because more and more reports were coming through that all of a sudden, people disappeared.
They were picked up by the Germans.
We left everything behind us.
I had to leave everything behind me-- my toys, my-- most of my clothing.
I had very little clothing left when we left.
Well, bit by bit, we brought it, actually, over before we went into hiding, come to think of it.
We would come back again and then take some more clothing with us.
But we couldn't take [LAUGHS] a suitcase or a-- or a big sack.
Yeah, we could maybe take a shopping bag, you know, like we had gone shopping.
But that was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the most we could do.
No.
I had no toys as a kid.
I didn't have too many toys, but I had no toys.
The only thing I had was maybe some paper and-- to write on, with a pencil.
And even that was limited.
Didn't-- I had literally nothing.
I would sit there in what you might call our underground place, and kind of look out of the window, make sure that nobody could see me.
I would look through the-- the curtains.
And just do nothing.
We had a radio and I would listen to the radio.

And we would listen to Hitler when he would speak, which of course was not encouraging, because his speeches were-- you know, as much as I could understand from it-- were not encouraging to listen to at all.
But anyhow, my-- when we moved in there, we moved-- we moved into hiding-- my father, my mother, my sister, and I were there.
And a few months later, things got-- I had a-- had a grandmother, an aunt, her daughter, and an uncle, and they were staying-- had an-- another uncle of mine who was married to a Christian lady.
And therefore, the mixed marriage was not subject to the Nazi persecution.
They were staying there.
But it was getting too dangerous for them to stay there.
So my father said, well you better come over here.
Uncle Bob.
Actually, Uncle Moses.
His name was Rubens.
This was all the Rubens family.
So my grandmother-- my grandfather had already passed away before the war.
So I had an aunt, Auntie-- Auntie Mina, her daughter, which was Rosetta, my grandmother, and my uncle, who was referred to as Uncle Bob, who also came all to live with us in a very small area.
I have a drawing somewhere.
I can show you that later.
And we lived all together in those few rooms that we had.
Well, there were-- there was a living room, there was a bedroom, a kitchen, and another little room.
I slept in the big bedroom, where my parents were.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
That's what it must have been.
I had a little bed, tucked, again, in a corner, where I would-- where I would sleep.
My sister, I think, she had a little room on the side.
And then the rest of the family was living in the living room, where they were make beds at night.
I guess when you put a lot of people together in a small area, where everybody knows that they have no place to go, everybody tries to be as cooperative as possible.
There were occasional flare ups and occasional difficulties, but it was all right.
We managed.
I remember we often really had pleasant evenings, because my father would-- had bought a little eight-millimeter projector.
And there was a rental place where-- in Rotterdam, where you could, through the mail, rent movies.
So we would rent movies.
It's like blockbuster night.
And nobody knew how to work the projector.
So my father said, can you work the projector?
And I took one look at it and the writing instructions.
I said, yeah.
I was seven years old.
It was a piece of cake.
The film would break.
I would splice the film together like I'd done it all my life.
And I was the projectionist, and we would run movies after movies.
Well, not movies after movies.
Once a week, we had movie night.
Let me say it like that.
And they were black and white, and they were a Stan Laurel and Oliver Hardy like things.
So, yea, we would-- that was our entertainment of the week.
We had Mrs.-- the lady that we had rented the place from, that had accommodated us and put us underground, was our shopping lady.
Mrs.
Eusman would go and do the shopping for us.
And at night time, my father and I-- I think the curfew was at 10:00-- but between 8:00 and 10:00, we would go for a walk, after it would get dark.
And here and there, we would have little places where the underground had told us that they might be able to pick up some food.
So we would pick up some [NON-ENGLISH] at one place, and some other stuff at another place.
And we had a carpenter friend, who was-- helped us get extra food whenever-- whatever we could get.
Of course, the underground was helping us with the-- the [INAUDIBLE] system-- how do you call that-- that just-- we could only buy so much food.
And because we were not registered, we didn't have the distribution-- [INAUDIBLE] in order to buy food.
So we had to buy food on the black market to a degree.
And we-- anyhow, we got by.
We got by as it was.
It wasn't easy, but in those days, there was still a fair amount of food around.
It was-- when we talk about '43, '44, that was the horror time, when there literally was no food anywhere.
But they got by on that.
And then, my father was talking to the carpenter.
And he came over and said, well, he said, you know, if there ever is a raid, then-- and they would come into the house-- they just would break the door down-- kick it in.
And maybe you should create a hiding place.
So he built two new walls in-- in the two connecting rooms, which were connected with sliding doors.
The sliding doors were taken out, and built an extra wall, which was about, oh, about this wide.
I'd say about four feet wide.
And made the room smaller by exactly the size of a wardrobe, by which you could take the entire inside panel away, and close that panel from the inside in the hiding place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Before we went into hiding, my father had bought a little metal cash box, because he had some shares.
And so-- my father was such a klutz, he couldn't even open up a little keyed cash box.
So I had to show him how to open it.
And apparently I was the person that was kind of handy in mechanical things, and he was exactly the opposite.
So in the cash box were some of his securities, that he had bought and saved over the years, and had got them out of the bank.
I don't know where he kept them, but he kept them first in a hiding place, [INAUDIBLE] in the cellar.
There was a cellar there.
And I know the place still, and I was the one that always had to grab it, because you would get dirty.
He didn't want to get dirty in order to grab it if necessary.
And then we would-- then we took that, and we put a little cash box into the hiding place.
We lived there-- and maybe I should carry on-- we lived in the hiding place till-- for about eight or nine months.
This was [INAUDIBLE] yeah-- '42.
Well, there was a shopping gallery underneath.
And that's why we were actually on the second level, above this gallery.

It was a high-- there were high buildings, in a way-- not that high-- because you would go up one flight of stairs, and that would be the area where our lady that had given us the space was living, Mrs.
Eusman, who lived there with her brother.
And then you could go up one flight higher, which would bring you in the attic.
In the attic was also living a man and a woman.
I think they were Polish.
They were a couple.
I don't think they were married.
They were living together in one little room.
And when you opened a little window there, you were actually on top of the roof.
From there you could step virtually into the gutter of the roofing system.
So they were living in the attic.
I never found out what they were.
But for some reason, things were not entirely kosher because he was hiding from the Germans too.
It could be that he was a criminal, that he had done some criminal things.
But that I don't know.
There were a number of people living in that place.
I would-- I would, from time to time, I would go up to their-- to their room and say hello and sit with them.
They were always very nice to me because, you know, just a little kid that they would talk to from time to time-- gave a little bit of diversion for them.
And we would listen to the radio.
In those days, we didn't have regular radio.
We only had what is called distribution radio, which was a knob that was controlled by the Germans.
You turn a knob and you had [GERMAN] one and [GERMAN] two.
That was all you could listen to.
And--
Oh yeah.
I think they were fully aware of it.
But because they were in the same boat, somehow, they were hiding from the Germans, we felt that we were safe with them.
We didn't know that they were there until we moved in.
But they were all the way on top in the attic, and they kept as quiet as a mouse.
But she was a blonde woman and she spoke German, and she would go out from time to time to buy some food for themselves.
But--
No, absolutely not.
That was it.
That was it.
Yeah.
So we were living there for a while, and everything seemed to be OK.
The hiding place was in place.
And then, one day, there was a raid.
And we were living very close to the bridge that went over the [INAUDIBLE], a canal.
And we were watching and ready to go into the hiding place, that if they would ring the bell they won't inspect.
Because in the raid, they would go from door to door to door and inspect.
And we knew that there was no way to escape them, unless we got in time into the hiding place.
We-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- we watched them.
And lit--
We watched the Germans setting up barricades and blocking the roads.
And we watched them, and at first, they were on one side of the canal-- the right side of the canal from our position, away from us.
And we saw them hauling people out of homes.
And it was a terrible experience, just watching how they were dragging people out of the homes and putting them in trucks.
And then the trucks would drive away with all the people.
And they would be-- it was just-- the fear in the house was-- you could have cut it with a knife, because we we're afraid that they were going to continue.
Luckily, they stopped right at that bridge, and they didn't go into the west end and check the west end out.
So we cleared that hurdle, and we all sighed with relief, ay yi yi, thank God and whatever more.
So we got-- we did not get caught in that crunch or raid-- as you-- they called it Razia-- in that raid, as you might say.

And then, yeah, a few months later-- I don't exactly remember when-- I think it was around about September of '42 or so, it must have been-- we were all, after-- after dinner we were just sitting around a table, talking in general, and, yeah, nothing much happening.
And--
I didn't go to school.
No, I didn't go to school.
It's a very good question.
And-- but how is it to live like that?
It was a matter of no choice.
You-- I know I couldn't go outside because the Germans would pick me up.
If I was-- I would be walking by myself, they might-- somebody might stop me and say, hey, what are you doing here?
There was curfew at night.
At 10:00, nobody could be out on the street.
I-- I still don't remember how I got through it, except I know one of the things.
Whenever I am in a condominium, or in an apartment, I can't wait to get out of the place, because I feel so claustrophobic.
I-- it just drives me bananas to be in small spaces, because I was literally closed up in this small area-- nothing to do except maybe some reading.
There was no school.
I had no friends.
I could listen to some radio, which became boring after a while.
Because yeah, you listen to a radio and you can still do other things, right?
But you brought-- you had books there?
We had some books, yeah.

The only thing that kept me going-- that seemed to keep me going at night is that I had always music going through my head, and I would go to bed maybe at 8:30, 9:00 and I would lay in bed, and I would think about music and all kinds of musical-- I had asked my father when I was-- I was eight years old.
That's when the war-- about that time.
I said I would like-- we were living right behind the conservatory, in the [INAUDIBLE].
And I had asked my father if I could go to the conservatory instead of going to school.
And my father had looked at me with horror-stricken eyes and had sworn, with almost foam coming out of his mouth, over my dead body will you go to the conservatory.
No way.
You're going to be a businessman, and not a frigging musician.
But the thing that seemed to always happen is I would go to bed and I would have music going through my head.
And I guess that kept me sane.
It kept me from going bananas.
I don't know where the music came from, but I had always music going through my head.

Later on in years, I find-- I have found out that I would turn off the mu-- I would turn off the radio, because the music, which still goes through my head today, is interfering with the music on the radio.
So I would turn the radio off.
But anyhow, back to-- to where was I?
Help me here.
I-- We-- we got betrayed.
One mor-- one evening-- Actually, we were talking about the fact that the Germans didn't cross the bridge.
You were safe for a long time?
Yeah.
We were safe for a number of months.
But then, the people in Holland were told that if they could give the name of a hiding place of a Jew, they would get something like 100 guilders.
Well hey, there were eight people there.
That would mean 800 guilders, right?
So Mrs.
Eusman-- because that's the only explanation we can-- we have for it-- went to the Germans, and told them to-- hey.
There's eight Jews there.
And one evening, we were sitting around a table.
It's about 8:00.
And it was timed well, because there was a pull-cord to open the door.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was near the pull-cord.
The bell rang.
She pulled the cord.
Which would open the downstairs front door--
To the street--
From the street.
And two or three guys came at tremendous speed up the stairway, because I remember the [INAUDIBLE], the sound of the steps, of somebody running with tremendous speed, running up the stairway.
And barging into the room where we were all sitting-- and my father was sitting with his back towards the window.
And I was standing behind the back.
I think we were about ready to go and start to-- some movies.
And my-- my father was standing there, and he sees those guys barging in, and he knew immediately that it was the SS Polizei.
And they said, we are the SS Polizei.
And you are Jew Rubens.
And you are so and so and you are so and so.
And you are under the equivalent of arrest.
We will take you.
One of the guys walked immediately into the hiding place, picked up the little cash box with the securities-- took that.
Then my father was-- he was standing up, and I was standing behind my father.
And he goes from-- he goes over his hip pocket very carefully, very suave-like.
And I'm standing behind me.
And he had-- he always carried big wads of money on him, because he couldn't go to the bank, right?
So he had a lot of money on him.
And he pulls the money out of it and pushes the money to me.
Like he was going like-- like this.
And I'm standing there for a moment.
And I said, what does he want?
He couldn't talk to me.
And [INAUDIBLE] what does he want?
[INAUDIBLE] I'm this eight-year-old kid.
I'm not that bright yet.
I had no experience in these things.
And it all of a sudden dawns on me that he wants me to take care of this money.
So I kind of walked over to him and grabbed the money, and tucked it into my-- into my belt, because my pockets weren't big enough to take this wad of money.
And I didn't-- I understood I didn't want it to show.
Anyhow, we-- everybody-- my Uncle Bob happened to be one flight up the stairs.

And I still could see him coming, sneaking, hand-over-hand, very carefully down one flight of stair, and running down the little hallway, and fleeing out the double stairs, out of the hiding place, and disappearing into the night.
He was not picked up.

But my grandfa-- my grandmother, my aunt, my cousin, my father, my mother, my sister, and myself-- we were taken into the truck and brought to the theater-- the Dutch-- or the Jewish theater, as it was called-- the [INAUDIBLE] where everybody was being held until they would put them into cattle cars and bring them to Westerbork.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was put into a building, because I was a young child, which was called the creche.
Why they called it the creche, I don't know.
I don't think anybody has ever figured that out.
But it was called the creche, spelled C-R-E-C-H-E.
And in this children holding area, I came in-- in the middle of the night, because it took quite a while before we finally were, literally, processed.
So I get there somewhere around about midnight.
We get into this-- I get in this big room, and somebody walks up to me and says, yeah.
Go and take that bunk.
So I'm being given a bunk is this big hole where all kinds of children were sleeping.
And very unhappy with myself and very scared, I went in there, and I-- I went to sleep.
Then during the daytime, around about 2:00 in the afternoon, we would get something to eat in the morning and something in the afternoon, I think.
I remember that.
We-- I was not that hungry.
And we would-- we would then go in the afternoon and spend two hours with our parents in the theater.
And then by 4:00, we were all garrisoned, or all cordoned off and told to go back to the creche, where we would then get maybe more food and-- and go to sleep.
Yeah.
As far as I remember, it was two stories.
There was a main lower area and there was a backyard.
There was a big area-- a big room, you might call it-- maybe about, oh, about maybe 10 times the size of this room, where the children were sleeping.
There must have been some kind of a kitchen area.
And then upstairs were rooms and the kids would kind of hang around there-- play with whatever little-- whatever was available.
I don't remember that much of the place because it was a very traumatic experience being there, being picked up by the Germans.
The atmosphere was, again, loaded with fear, fear, fear.
What is going to happen to us next?
Who took care of you in that place?
There were a number of people from the Jewish council.
They were made in charge by the Germans to take care of the children.
And you see, the-- the Germans figured that, hey, the children are not going to leave.
The young children are not going to leave their parents, and the parents are not going to leave their children.
So by splitting them up, everybody is going to stay put where they are.
Except my sister, who was a few years older than I was, had found that, through one side door, she could reach a little hallway that would go to a door that went right out to the street.
And she told my father about this.
And my father said, well, get out and go to our prearranged place, where we were going to meet if anything like this would-- would happen.
That was the carpenter, Thyssen, who had built the double wall, who was our safe house if we ever would-- if ever anything would happen.
T-H-Y-S-S-E-N.
I don't know his first name, because he was always referred to as Mr.
Thyssen.
[LAUGHS] You know the formalities in Holland.
We don't work on first name.
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ronny just-- literally just walked out of that side door and disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day, I went to my father, and said, I don't where Ronny is.
She's gone.
And my father said, that's good.
That's good.
But I hadn't-- didn't know how she had gotten away.
People were watching us, and I was afraid to do almost anything, you might say.
And one day I got very scared because I was-- I moved a standing electrical lamp.
And the electrical lamp was connected to the electricity, and there was a short circuit.
And the steel bar-- and I almost electrocuted myself.
Fortunately, I walked backwards.
And as I walked backwards, by the impact, I pulled the connection out of the-- out of the-- out of the wall.
And because of that, I'm still here to talk about it, because I was totally-- I couldn't move.
Anyhow, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where was I?
I-- My sister was gone.
My father says, well, I've got to get you out of here also.
And we're getting now to the day that we were told, OK.
Tomorrow, in the evening, you'll be transported to a central station to go to be transported to the concentration camp, Westerbork.
So now we have the day that my father says, what am I going to do?
My grandmother is there, my aunt, my uncle.
And everybody is in a-- is basically is in a total panic.
